serving.natural_language_portion: One medium raw onion weighs approximately 110 g and is commonly used whole, sliced, or diced as an ingredient in cooking or eaten raw in salads.

nutrient_source.title: Onion, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw onion (cr_id F14040010). Cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11282 (Onions, raw) for secondary validation of saturated fat, potassium, vitamin C, and additional micronutrients.

gi_source.title: Onion, raw — Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load values from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Raw onion has a published GI of approximately 10 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 110 g serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (10 × 9.34 × 1.10) / 100 ≈ 1.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (4 mg/100 g) and quercetin-mediated vasodilation
hypertension.guidance: Raw onion is highly suitable for hypertensive individuals: sodium content is negligible at 4 mg/100 g, potassium is 146 mg/100 g supporting favourable Na:K ratio, and quercetin — a flavonoid abundant in onion — has demonstrated clinically significant blood-pressure-lowering and vasodilatory effects consistent with DASH dietary principles.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI ≈ 10), moderate fibre, and quercetin/chromium insulin-sensitising activity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw onion is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes: its GI of approximately 10 produces minimal postprandial glucose excursion, dietary fibre (1.7 g/100 g) moderates carbohydrate absorption, and bioactive compounds quercetin and chromium have demonstrated insulin-sensitising properties in ADA-aligned research, supporting glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and cardioprotective quercetin and organosulfur compounds
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw onion is cardioprotective: it contains negligible saturated fat (0.04 g/100 g), zero cholesterol, and is rich in quercetin and organosulfur compounds (allicin precursors) that have demonstrated anti-platelet aggregation, LDL-oxidation inhibition, and endothelial protective effects in peer-reviewed cardiovascular research.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Quercetin anti-inflammatory activity and vitamin C antioxidant support
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Raw onion contains quercetin, a polyphenol with documented inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis pathophysiology, and vitamin C (7.4 mg/100 g) providing antioxidant support; it is generally well-tolerated and may offer modest anti-inflammatory benefit, though it should not replace disease-modifying therapy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Raw onion (Allium cepa) is a pungent, crisp bulb vegetable consumed worldwide in its uncooked state as a salad ingredient, condiment, or flavour base. It has a sharp, slightly sweet flavour that mellows with slicing and exposure to air. Nutritionally, raw onion is low in calories (40 kcal/100 g), very low in fat (0.1 g/100 g), and provides moderate carbohydrates (9.34 g/100 g) with a very low glycemic index of approximately 10, making it suitable for blood sugar management. It supplies dietary fibre (1.7 g/100 g), calcium (23 mg/100 g), iron (0.21 mg/100 g), potassium (146 mg/100 g), vitamin C (7.4 mg/100 g), and folate (19 mcg/100 g). Raw onion is particularly notable for its high quercetin content — a flavonoid with documented anti-inflammatory, vasodilatory, and insulin-sensitising properties — and organosulfur compounds that confer cardiovascular and antimicrobial benefits. It is naturally sodium-free (4 mg/100 g), cholesterol-free, and suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, kosher, and gluten-free diets. In clinical nutrition, raw onion is recommended for hypertension (DASH diet compatible), Type 2 Diabetes management, and cardiovascular disease prevention. It is a staple ingredient across Singaporean, South Asian, Southeast Asian, Middle Eastern, and Western cuisines.
common_user_queries: Is raw onion good for diabetes?
Can I eat onion if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a raw onion?
What are the health benefits of raw onion?
How much fibre does onion have?
Is onion suitable for a vegan diet?
What vitamins are in raw onion?
Is onion good for heart health?

confidence_reason: Primary nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14040010, confidence 0.95). Secondary micronutrient values cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11282 (Onions, raw). GI value sourced from University of Sydney GI Database. All sources are approved under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
notes: Core macros are all non-zero and require no zero-justification. Saturated fat (0.04 g), monounsaturated fat (0.01 g), and polyunsaturated fat (0.02 g) values are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11282 (Onions, raw) as HPB source does not report fatty acid fractions; this adaptation is documented here. Trans fat is 0.0 g — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg — confirmed zero for plant food per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin A is 0.0 mcg RAE — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 mcg — naturally zero as this is a plant food with no animal-derived components. Vitamin D is 0.0 mcg — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy. Potassium (146 mg), vitamin C (7.4 mg), vitamin B1 (0.05 mg), vitamin B2 (0.03 mg), vitamin B3 (0.12 mg), vitamin B6 (0.12 mg), folate (19 mcg DFE), vitamin E (0.02 mg), phosphorus (29 mg), magnesium (10 mg), zinc (0.17 mg), selenium (0.5 mcg), and water (89.11 g) are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 11282. Raw onion is a generic food record; no cuisine-specific multilingual naming is required (G6 not triggered). The rheumatoid_arthritis entry in the input had an internally contradictory suitable=false flag paired with a reason citing quercetin as anti-inflammatory; this has been corrected to suitable=true with appropriate clinical nuance in additional_conditions. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and FoodEx2 code A00LC are defensible matches; SNOMED CT code 260188000 is used for the substance concept. Reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code A00LC against the current EFSA FoodEx2 release if this record is used in EU regulatory contexts.
